source: Approved revised diff table DT-2026-05-13-alignment-loop-contract during user brainstorming on 2026-05-13.
summary: Accepted alignment-loop direction: model each compiler loop as cycle builds validated by a pure gateway, add an explicit planning loop before implementation, and make graph traceability a lean hot index over source-backed alignment rather than the alignment engine itself.

diff_table.current_state: Compiler docs model three loops: feedback -> documentation -> implementation. Documentation may create or update roadmap work directly.
diff_table.desired_state: Introduce an explicit planning loop between documentation and implementation: feedback -> documentation -> planning -> implementation. Documentation updates KB; planning aligns roadmap tasks, acceptance criteria, TDD plan, code/test candidate paths, and implementation scope.
diff_table.rationale: Separating KB alignment from roadmap alignment keeps CDD boundaries clear and avoids hiding planning semantics inside documentation updates.
diff_table.risk: Medium: adds a loop and new build kind, so docs and tooling must stay lean to avoid ceremony.
alternatives: Keep roadmap mutation in documentation loop but make planning a documentation subphase.
Do not add a build kind; add planning fields to documentation_build.

diff_table.current_state: Validation gateway docs say it decides whether a loop can end and whether the next loop can consume the produced build.
diff_table.desired_state: Simplify gateway responsibility to one pure action: validate a cycle build against its embedded policy, source refs, and evidence, returning pass/fail/block. Compilers create builds; gateway evaluates builds; gateway does not invent requirements or own planning.
diff_table.rationale: A single-purpose gateway is easier to reason about and makes handoffs reproducible across fresh sessions.
diff_table.risk: Low/medium: existing docs and tool names can adapt without major behavior break, but validator profiles need clearer wording.
alternatives: Keep gateway as both exit-criteria definer and validator.
Split gateway into preflight and close gates.

diff_table.current_state: Builds are loop handoff contracts, but cycle semantics are implicit and validation criteria are spread across loop docs, validation reports, and compiler behavior.
diff_table.desired_state: Define an alignment cycle as one build attempt. Each cycle build carries cycle metadata, supersedes links, policy profile, exit criteria, requirement IDs, evidence mapping, risks, open questions, non-goals, and agent assessment. Fail/block creates a later superseding cycle build; pass allows downstream consumption.
diff_table.rationale: Cycle-as-build removes a separate concept tree, gives fresh sessions one artifact to consume, and keeps the gateway pure.
diff_table.risk: Medium: if every micro-step becomes a cycle, artifact spam grows; policy should only create cycles at loop-level alignment attempts, not every tool call or TDD micro-step.
alternatives: Persist cycles under a new .codewiki/cycles tree.
Keep cycles chat-only and rely on builds without explicit metadata.

diff_table.current_state: Feedback builds require approved diff-table rows and capture decisions, assumptions, open questions, non-goals, and risks. The agent's first-principles critique is described but not a first-class field.
diff_table.desired_state: Make feedback-loop alignment explicit: diff table records current state vs desired state; below it the agent gives a blunt first-principles assessment, disagreements, simpler alternatives, assumptions, and focused questions. The resulting feedback_build records the accepted assessment and approved rows.
diff_table.rationale: The feedback loop is where poor requirements should be challenged before they become KB, roadmap, tests, or code drift.
diff_table.risk: Low: mostly contract/documentation, but agents must avoid performative critique and keep assessments actionable.
alternatives: Leave agent assessment as freeform notes only.
Move critique into validation rather than feedback compiler.

diff_table.current_state: Graph/state reports green, but product-story traceability can be weak because requirement-to-KB-to-plan-to-test/code coverage is not first-class. The graph also persists enough cold/derived information that it can look like the alignment engine instead of a generated index over source truth.
diff_table.desired_state: Make alignment source from cycle builds, KB, planning builds, roadmap tasks, tests/code, and validation evidence. Make the graph a lean hot state machine and index over those truths: route next loop, expose latest active/superseding cycle builds, stale inputs, unconsumed handoffs, active claims, fail/block validation, and a compact requirement traceability matrix. The graph reports traceability gaps; it does not own requirements or solve alignment by itself. Cold history/archive detail remains explicit-on-demand.
diff_table.rationale: Alignment is a chain of source-backed handoffs, not a big graph. Requirement IDs should connect feedback -> KB -> planning -> tests/code -> implementation evidence -> validation, while the graph stays efficient and points agents to exact truth sources.
diff_table.risk: Medium/high: requires careful design to avoid turning traceability into noisy bureaucracy or hiding useful audit data. The guardrail is hot-only graph state plus on-demand archive/audit expansion.
alternatives: Only fix freshness bugs in current graph.
Add traceability as a separate generated view outside index_graph.json.
Keep traceability only in builds and do not surface it in the graph.

diff_table.current_state: Implementation loop consumes documentation_build and roadmap work, optionally splits tester/builder evidence, and produces implementation_build. TDD is implied in evidence but not framed as the loop contract.
diff_table.desired_state: Document implementation as a TDD-aligned loop: consume planning_build, create/record failing tests or test-design evidence first where practical, implement code to pass, map tests/code/checks to requirement IDs, then produce implementation_build for validation/publication.
diff_table.rationale: CDD defines context and plan; TDD proves behavior. Requirement-to-test evidence is the practical anti-drift mechanism before code publication.
diff_table.risk: Medium: strict TDD can be impractical for docs/config/refactor tasks; policy should allow justified test-design evidence or explicit non-goal exceptions.
alternatives: Keep TDD as recommended practice only.
Require separate tester and builder agents for all implementation tasks.

accepted_decisions.summary: Adopt feedback -> documentation -> planning -> implementation as the intended alignment flow, with planning promoted to a first-class loop and planning_build proposed as the roadmap-alignment handoff.
Treat each loop cycle as a build attempt; a failed or blocked cycle is superseded by a later cycle build rather than represented by a separate long-lived cycle tree.
Simplify the validation gateway to a pure evaluator: validate a submitted build against embedded policy, source refs, and evidence; return pass, fail, or block.
Keep requirements and alignment truth in source-backed artifacts: feedback builds, KB docs, planning builds, roadmap tasks, tests/code, implementation builds, and validation evidence.
Use requirement IDs and evidence mappings to trace user intent through KB, plan, tests, code, validation, and publication.
Keep the graph as a lean hot state machine/index that reports next loops and traceability gaps; the graph does not own requirements or replace source reads.
Frame implementation as TDD-aligned after planning: tests/test-design evidence first where practical, then code, then implementation_build closure evidence.

assumptions: The current request is a feedback-loop acceptance only; documentation updates come next through the documentation compiler.
No canonical KB, roadmap, or code changes should be made directly by the feedback loop beyond this accepted feedback build and pending diff-table runtime state.
A future implementation may require schema/tool updates because current tooling only has feedback, documentation, and implementation build kinds.
Planning loop semantics should stay lean and avoid duplicating full requirements in roadmap tasks.
Cycle builds should be created only for loop-level alignment attempts, not every tool call or every red/green micro-step.
open_questions: What exact fields should planning_build include beyond tasks, acceptance criteria, TDD plan, code/test candidates, and requirement coverage?
Should traceability live inside index_graph.json as a hot compact view, or should it be computed on-demand by the state API and only summarized in the graph?
Which existing docs should own the planning-loop contract: compilers.md, roadmap.md, builds.md, validation-gateway.md, graph.md, or a new planning.md system doc?
non_goals: Do not implement planning_build code or schema in this feedback build.
Do not create a separate .codewiki/cycles tree unless future evidence proves builds are insufficient.
Do not make the graph the source of requirements, policies, or validation truth.
Do not require strict TDD for documentation-only, config-only, or justified non-code tasks.

knowledge: Update .codewiki/kb/system/compilers.md for four-loop alignment flow and cycle-as-build compiler semantics.
Update .codewiki/kb/system/builds.md for planning_build and cycle metadata/policy/evidence mapping fields.
Update .codewiki/kb/system/validation-gateway.md for pure build-validation responsibility.
Update .codewiki/kb/system/roadmap.md for planning loop ownership of roadmap alignment.
Update .codewiki/kb/system/graph.md for lean hot state machine plus requirement traceability index role.
Update .codewiki/kb/lexicon.md for alignment cycle, planning_build, requirement ID, and traceability matrix terms.
Review skill docs for feedback/documentation/implementation/verify to align compiler routing language.
roadmap: Documentation compiler should create a roadmap task only after KB alignment decides the implementation delta for planning_build/schema/tooling support.
